intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

body.intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

draft.title: recipe app for people who hate cooking
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
recipe app for people who hate cooking

Building your first draft…

data.intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking prefer soft, approachable interfaces over clinical precision
facade.label: Warm, rounded corners
facade.acceptImplies: Explore playful, forgiving design language; test friendly typography and gentle color gradients
facade.rejectImplies: Investigate cleaner, more structured layouts; test sharper lines and higher contrast; consider minimalist aesthetic

evidence.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking prefer soft, approachable interfaces over clinical precision
evidence.implication: Explore playful, forgiving design language; test friendly typography and gentle color gradients

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking respond better to real food imagery than abstract symbols
facade.label: Ingredient photos, not icons
facade.acceptImplies: Show high-res food photography for every ingredient; users want tactile, recognizable visuals over minimal UI
facade.rejectImplies: Abstract ingredient symbols work fine; users don't need photo realism to understand what they're cooking with

facade.hypothesis: For people who hate cooking, a bright, encouraging tone with quick-win UI patterns (big buttons, progress indicators, celebration feedback) might reduce friction better than minimalist restraint.
facade.axisTargeted: Warm vs Cool / Energetic vs Calm
facade.label: Cheerful shortcuts
facade.acceptImplies: User gravitates toward conversational, emoji-friendly, reward-driven interface. Probably values motivation and momentum over elegance.
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers understated, no-nonsense aesthetic. Likely wants efficiency without cheerleading. May find enthusiasm patronizing.

facade.hypothesis: Users hate cooking apps that demand navigation; showing recipes instantly (no taps) reduces friction for people who resent cooking
facade.label: Instant recipe pop-up
facade.acceptImplies: User values speed and minimal steps; tolerates overlay UI if it saves cognitive load
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers traditional navigation; finds overlays intrusive; wants clear page hierarchy even if it adds taps

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking respond better to irreverent, self-aware voice over neutral instruction—they want validation, not judgment
facade.label: Snarky tone
facade.acceptImplies: The app should acknowledge user frustration with cooking, use humor to defuse kitchen anxiety, feel like a friend who gets it rather than a wellness influencer
facade.rejectImplies: The app should stay neutral, instructional, and professional—focus on simplicity and clarity over personality or attitude

facade.hypothesis: For a 'hate cooking' audience, hiding secondary navigation frees up mental space and reduces overwhelm—tabs keep ingredients/steps visible without constant menu switching.
facade.axisTargeted: navigation density and screen real estate allocation
facade.label: Collapsed sidebar with tabs
facade.acceptImplies: User wants compact navigation; likely values quick access to core features (search, saved recipes, timer) over discoverability of app depth.
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers always-visible navigation or a different spatial model (full sidebar, bottom nav, or single-view list). May indicate they want to browse or need wayfinding support.

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking prefer simplified, bold color palettes over photorealistic food imagery
facade.label: Bright, flat colors
facade.acceptImplies: Test vibrant solid colors, minimize food photography, explore illustrative or icon-based recipe presentation
facade.rejectImplies: Users may want depth cues and realistic visual anchors; explore muted palettes, photography, or textured surfaces

axes.label: Whether user wants visual simplicity and minimal ingredient lists vs. rich food photography and detailed plating inspiration
Whether user prefers step-by-step guided workflows vs. ingredient-first browsing and flexible navigation
Whether user responds to encouraging, motivational tone vs. irreverent, humorous tone that validates cooking aversion
Whether user wants calm, meditative cooking experience vs. quick-win, dopamine-hit satisfaction from easy wins
Whether user wants minimal info (just ingredients and time) vs. detailed context (why this works, substitutions, tips)
Whether user prefers instant gratification with no animations vs. satisfying micro-interactions that celebrate small progress

draft.title: No-Fuss Recipes
draft.summary: A recipe app designed for people who want delicious meals with minimal cooking complexity. Focuses on quick, simple ingredients and straightforward instructions.
draft.html:
No-Fuss Recipes

Simple meals, minimal effort

Under 15 min
5 ingredients
No oven
One pan

Quick Picks

Garlic Butter Pasta

12 min 4 ingredients Stovetop

Pasta tossed with butter, garlic, and fresh parsley. No sauce, no fuss.

Sheet Pan Chicken

25 min 6 ingredients Oven

Chicken thighs with roasted vegetables. Everything on one pan.

Egg Fried Rice

10 min 5 ingredients Stovetop

Leftover rice, eggs, and soy sauce. Ready in minutes.
